Buy a Domain (Google Domains) and Point It at Your Blog on GitHub Pages With Cloudflare
This post is historical reference. Google Domains was discontinued in 2023 and transferred to Squarespace. For new domain purchases today, check out my newer post:
Google Domains Shutdown: Set Up Your Cloudflare Domain Step by Step
The GitHub Pages + Cloudflare setup portion below is still applicable if you already have a domain (whether from Google Domains, Squarespace, Cloudflare, or elsewhere).

Plenty of people host their tech blogs on GitHub Pages or GitLab Pages and want a custom URL for it. This post walks through buying a domain (originally on Google Domains), pointing it at GitHub Pages, and putting Cloudflare in front for free SSL/acceleration.
Why bother with a custom domain?
Let me start with why you'd pay for a domain when GitHub gives you a free URL.
GitHub Pages and GitLab Pages give you a URL based on your username — if your GitHub handle is
. That's the default URL for your blog or project portfolio.
What's bad about that? A few things:
If the host has an outage, your site dies — your URL points at their infrastructure
If the host kills the feature, you lose your SEO equity
The URL isn't memorable, and it's tied to your platform identity

. If you move to your own hosting later, your SEO juice doesn't follow — anyone who linked to your old URL is broken. Free URLs are fine for project portfolios and quick projects, but for a long-term blog, get a domain.

In the repo's Settings → Pages → "Custom domain" — enter your purchased domain and click Save:
You'll probably see this error first:

is improperly configured
Domain's DNS record could not be retrieved. For more information, see documentation (InvalidDNSError).
Ignore it — give it a few minutes and the domain will start resolving:
You'll also see GitHub auto-added a
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
That file tells GitHub Pages your custom domain. GitHub Pages also issues a free SSL cert automatically.
Once you switch GitHub Pages' Custom domain to your custom URL, all your links should use the new domain. Anyone visiting the old
URL gets auto-redirected to the new one with a 301 (so SEO is preserved).
GitHub Pages already gives you free SSL — so why add Cloudflare? Because Cloudflare adds free:
…all on the free tier. Optional but worth it for any production site.
and register. After login, you'll see this — click "Add a Site":
(Cloudflare has localized UIs — switch language in account settings if you prefer.)
You'll land on the plan picker. Scroll down to find "Free":
Free plan covers a lot of features already:
Now we need to switch the domain's DNS over to Cloudflare so they can handle SSL and the other features. Add a DNS record — match the screenshot:
and the target should be your original GitHub Pages URL (e.g.,
Save these — you'll need them next.
Back at your registrar (was Google Domains, now wherever your domain lives), find DNS settings → "Custom nameservers":
Enter the two Cloudflare nameservers and save:
Click "Switch to these settings" to actually apply:
If successful, you'll see "These DNS settings are valid. Changes will publish immediately but may take some time to take effect", and the heading changes to "Custom nameservers (in use)".
Back at Cloudflare, click "Done, check nameservers":
Cloudflare will verify your nameservers — takes a bit. You'll get an email confirming activation:
Everything else in Cloudflare is yours to explore — the localized UI makes it pretty discoverable.
One thing that'll bite you — after setting up Cloudflare, your domain might stop working after a while. The default SSL/TLS mode is "Flexible", which can cause this error in some browsers:
Fix — Cloudflare dashboard → SSL/TLS → change SSL/TLS mode to
Now your site should work again immediately.
